---
title: "Agentic AI for Academic Research"
subtitle: "A Watch-Along Seminar for Business-School Faculty"
author: "Pedro H. C. Sant'Anna"
institute: "Emory University · Visiting Notre Dame Mendoza"
date: 2026-04-27
date-format: "MMMM YYYY"
format:
  clean-revealjs:
    theme: claude-mini.scss
    logo: ../Figures/emory.png
    footer: "Claude-Mini · Notre Dame Watch-Along · Pedro H. C. Sant'Anna"
    slide-number: true
    chalkboard: true
    preview-links: auto
    transition: fade
    transition-speed: fast
    width: 1600
    height: 900
    margin: 0.05
---

```{r}
#| include: false
library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)
library(tidyr)

# Brand colors — Emory primary, Anthropic accent
emoryblue   <- "#002B7F"
emorygold   <- "#B9975B"
emoryyellow <- "#F2A900"
claudeorange <- "#cc785c"   # Anthropic / Claude brand accent — used for "agent" callouts

# Theme for plots
theme_claude_mini <- theme_minimal(base_size = 14) +
  theme(
    plot.title    = element_text(color = emoryblue, face = "bold", size = 16),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(color = "gray40", size = 12),
    axis.title    = element_text(color = emoryblue),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    legend.position = "bottom"
  )
```

# Welcome {.emoryblue}

## A 4-hour Watch-Along

::: {.incremental}
- **Goal**: by the end, you'll know whether to adopt this for your next paper, class, or RA workflow.
- **Format**: I demonstrate; you observe. Two 10-minute breaks. Q&A throughout.
- **Take-home**: a curated repo (`Claude-Mini`) you can clone tonight, and a working starter template (`claude-code-my-workflow`) you can fork.
:::

## What this is *not*

- Not a sales pitch.
- Not a tutorial on writing Python or installing software.
- Not "AI will write your paper." (Claude won't, and shouldn't.)

## What it *is*

A demonstration of how a research workflow built on top of agentic AI:

1. Plans before it acts.
2. Reviews its own work with multiple specialized agents.
3. Verifies its output before shipping.
4. Survives across sessions and across collaborators.

## Roadmap (4 hours, watch-along)

| Part | Topic | Time |
|------|-------|------|
| 1 | Agentic AI + the CLAUDE.md control center | 40 min |
| 2 | End-to-end demo: improve a manuscript section | 30 min |
| 3 | Building reusable skills (paper exercise) | 30 min |
| — | **Break** | 10 min |
| 4 | Orchestration + multi-agent systems | 25 min |
| 5 | Research + replication (live demo) | 45 min |
| — | **Break** | 10 min |
| 6 | Refereeing + review simulation (live demo) | 45 min |
| 7 | Closing + cross-tool strategies | 25 min |

---

# Part 1 — Agentic AI + CLAUDE.md {.claudeorange #part-1}

## The premise of this part {.compact}

::: {.incremental}
- The most useful thing a researcher writes in this stack is **not code**. It's a one-page file called `CLAUDE.md`.
- Everything else — skills, rules, hooks, multi-agent review — derives from it.
- Forty minutes from now, you'll know exactly what to put in yours.
:::

## Why now? {.compact}

Three things changed in the last 18 months:

::: {.incremental}
- **Replication compliance is no longer optional.** AEA Data Editor since 2019; JF Disclosure Statement since 2023; JAR full-data archive expected by 2027. Desk rejects on package failures are rising.
- **Referee reports got harder, not easier.** Higher methods bar at *AER* / *JF* / *JAR*; pre-registration creep into observational work; "what would change my mind" becoming a referee-side norm.
- **Agentic AI matured fast.** "Tool-using" went from research preview (2024) to production (2025). The gap between *chatbot* and *agent* is now the same as the gap between *spreadsheet* and *programming language*.
:::

## Why this audience {.compact}

::: {.incremental}
- B-school faculty in **economics, finance, accounting** sit at the intersection of these three pressures.
- High-stakes papers, slow review cycles, replication-package compliance now part of the job, not a footnote.
- Most faculty I talk to use AI tools as **chat replacements** — better Google. That leaves 80% of the value on the table.
:::

. . .

The next 4 hours: see what's on that table.

## What I'm not going to do {.compact}

- ❌ Sell you on adopting this. (You'll evaluate yourself.)
- ❌ Teach you Python or Stata or R. (Use what you already use.)
- ❌ Promise that "AI will write your paper." (Claude won't — and shouldn't.)

## What I am going to do

::: {.incremental}
1. Show you a workflow built on top of agentic AI that **plans before acting, reviews its own work, and verifies before shipping**.
2. Demonstrate it live on real materials — manuscript improvement, replication audit, simulated peer review.
3. Hand you a repo (`Claude-Mini`) you can clone tonight and a parent template (`claude-code-my-workflow`) you can fork on Monday.
:::

# Tool landscape {.claudeorange}

## The three big players (April 2026) {.compact}

| | Claude Code | Codex (OpenAI) | Gemini CLI |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Best for** | Repo-context tasks, multi-file refactor, agent orchestration | Structured completion, focused narrow tasks | Multimodal (image / PDF heavy) |
| **File-system access** | Native (read/write/run) | Native | Native (newer) |
| **Subagent / multi-agent** | First-class | Via plugin | Limited |
| **Hooks / lifecycle events** | Yes (extensive) | Limited | No |
| **My experience** | Daily driver | Cross-tool audits | Occasional, multimodal-only |

## What this talk uses {.compact}

We'll be in **Claude Code** for everything live. Two reasons:

::: {.incremental}
1. The infrastructure (skills, rules, agents, hooks) is the most mature here in April 2026.
2. The cross-tool pattern at the end (Part 7) uses **Claude reviewing Codex output** — same primitives generalize.
:::

. . .

If you're already in another tool: most concepts port. The CLAUDE.md → instructions-file pattern works in Cursor (`.cursorrules`), Aider (`.aider.conf.yml`), and continue.dev (`.continue/config.json`). Nothing here is Claude-exclusive in *concept*.

## What "agentic" actually means {.compact}

A chatbot:

> *You*: "Edit the abstract to mention the panel-data dimension."
> *Bot*: "Here's a revision: [text]"
> *You*: copy → paste → save → repeat.

. . .

An agent:

> *You*: "Edit the abstract to mention the panel-data dimension."
> *Agent*: opens the file, reads the abstract, writes the edit, saves, runs the bibliography check, opens the result, reports back.

. . .

The difference isn't intelligence. It's **the ability to act** — and to verify it acted correctly.

## The four agentic primitives {.compact}

| Primitive | What it gives you |
|-----------|-------------------|
| **Tool use** | Read files, run commands, edit on disk, call APIs |
| **Planning** | Break a task into reviewable steps before acting |
| **Sub-agents** | Spawn focused workers (proofreader, code reviewer, fact-checker) in parallel |
| **Hooks** | Deterministic enforcement at lifecycle events (pre-commit, post-edit) |

. . .

The next four parts walk through these one at a time. Today's lecture in one sentence: **a workflow is the pattern that connects them**.

# CLAUDE.md as control center {.claudeorange}

## The single most important file in this stack

`CLAUDE.md` lives at your project root.

. . .

It loads automatically at the start of every Claude session.

. . .

It tells the agent **what your project is**, **what the rules are**, and **what good looks like** — *before* the agent does anything.

. . .

It's ~150 lines. It changes everything downstream.

## What's in mine {.compact}

```markdown
# CLAUDE.MD — Causal Panel Data Course

## Core Principles
- Plan first; verify after.
- Single source of truth: Beamer .tex is authoritative.
- Quality gates: nothing ships below 80/100.

## Folder Structure
- Slides/ — Beamer .tex
- Quarto/ — RevealJS .qmd
- scripts/R/ — analysis pipeline
- Bibliography_base.bib — centralized citations

## Commands
TEXINPUTS=../Preambles:$TEXINPUTS xelatex -interaction=nonstopmode file.tex
quarto render file.qmd
```

. . .

I'll open the actual file in a moment.

## What that gives Claude

::: {.incremental}
- **Folder structure** → knows where to put things, what to read
- **Commands** → knows the exact LaTeX flags, knows `quarto render` over `pandoc`
- **Quality thresholds** → won't ship below 80/100 score; will halt and ask
- **Source-of-truth contract** → won't edit Quarto without syncing Beamer
:::

## Live walkthrough — the actual `CLAUDE.md` {auto-animate=true}

> [In live demo: Pedro opens `claude-code-my-workflow/CLAUDE.md` in editor.
> Walks line by line. Audience sees that the file is **short**, **specific**,
> and **prescriptive about what Claude can't guess**.]

::: {.callout-tip}
## Demo prompt

After the tour: delete the `## Commands` section. Re-open Claude. Ask: "render the slides." Watch Claude guess `pandoc` (wrong tool). Restore the section. Ask again. Watch Claude pick `quarto render`. **The file is the difference.**
:::

## Three sections that always pull weight {.compact}

::: {.incremental}
1. **Commands you'd type** — exact CLI invocations Claude can't infer (build flags, tool versions, environment variables).
2. **Folder structure** — where things live, with one-line "what's here" notes per directory.
3. **Quality thresholds + source-of-truth contracts** — non-negotiables. "Never edit X without syncing Y."
:::

## Three sections that look helpful but aren't {.compact}

::: {.incremental}
- ❌ A list of every package you use. Claude knows R packages.
- ❌ "Write clean code." Claude knows.
- ❌ A re-summary of files Claude can read. Skip — Claude reads them.
:::

. . .

**Test:** if removing the line caused Claude to make a mistake, keep it. If not, cut it.

## The CLAUDE.md test, formalized {.compact}

```bash
# 1. Open CLAUDE.md
# 2. Comment out a line
# 3. Restart Claude session
# 4. Ask: "render the slides" / "run the analysis" / "fix this bug"
# 5. Did Claude get it wrong?
#    YES → keep the line
#    NO  → delete it
```

. . .

This isn't a metaphor — it's a debugging method. Run it on your own CLAUDE.md once a quarter.

# Effective CLAUDE.md {.claudeorange}

## ✅ Include / ❌ Exclude {.compact}

| ✅ Include | ❌ Exclude |
|-----------|-----------|
| Bash commands Claude can't guess | Standard language conventions |
| Code-style rules that differ from defaults | Detailed API docs (link to source) |
| Testing instructions + preferred runners | Self-evident practices ("write good code") |
| Repository etiquette (branch naming, PR conventions) | File-by-file descriptions |
| Architectural decisions specific to your project | Information that changes frequently |
| Developer-environment quirks (env vars, paths) | A summary of files Claude can already read |
| Common gotchas / non-obvious behaviors | Long explanations or tutorials |

## Specificity wins {.compact}

::: {.incremental}
- ❌ "Use good R code style."
- ✅ "Use `fixest::feols`, not `lm`. Cluster SE at firm level. `set.seed(20260427)` once at top of `00_run_all.R`."
:::

::: {.incremental}
- ❌ "Be careful with citations."
- ✅ "Citations: `\citep{key}` for parenthetical, `\citet{key}` for narrative. All keys must exist in `Bibliography_base.bib` before commit."
:::

## Specificity wins, part 2 {.compact}

::: {.incremental}
- ❌ "Write clean Python."
- ✅ "Use `pandas` for data, `pyarrow` for serialization. Type hints required for public functions. Black formatter. `pytest -xvs` is the test runner."
:::

::: {.incremental}
- ❌ "The folder structure is intuitive."
- ✅ "data/raw/ — never modified after download. data/clean/ — outputs of `02_clean.R` only. results/ — read-only, regenerated by `00_run_all.R`."
:::

## A finance example (your CLAUDE.md, draft 1) {.compact}

```markdown
# CLAUDE.MD — JF Submission

## Core Principles
- Default journal: JF.
- Replication policy: JF Disclosure Statement compliance.
- Standard error convention: cluster by firm + month.

## Data Sources
- WRDS: Compustat (2000-2023), CRSP (1990-2023)
- WRDS connection: see scripts/setup-wrds.R
- Sample restrictions: SIC 6000-6999 EXCLUDED (financials).

## Commands
- Estimation: Rscript scripts/02_main_regression.R
- Tables: scripts/03_format_tables.R writes to /tables/
- Replication audit: /audit-reproducibility paper.tex scripts/
```

. . .

That's enough. Stop there.

## An accounting example (your CLAUDE.md, draft 1) {.compact}

```markdown
# CLAUDE.MD — TAR Revision

## Core Principles
- Target: TAR R&R round 2.
- Referee 1 wants robustness to alternative event windows.
- Referee 2 wants placebo tests on non-event firms.

## Data
- I/B/E/S analyst data at scripts/data/ibes_clean.rds
- Audit Analytics restatements: data/aa_restate.rds
- Sample: 2010-2022, US-listed, non-financial.

## Workflow
- New analyses go to scripts/r2_response/ (NOT main scripts/).
- Each analysis: ONE script that writes ONE table to tables/r2/.
- Update response-letter.tex with mapping: analysis script -> referee point.
```

## Length budget {.compact}

::: {.incremental}
- **Aim for 100-200 lines.** That's where most working CLAUDE.md files live.
- **Hard ceiling: ~300.** Past that, important rules get lost.
- **If yours is over 300:** something belongs in a *rule file* (path-scoped), not the universal CLAUDE.md.
:::

. . .

We'll get to rule files in 5 minutes.

# Rules and hooks (preview) {.claudeorange}

## When CLAUDE.md gets too long {.compact}

The fix: **path-scoped rule files** under `.claude/rules/`.

::: {.incremental}
- A rule file is just like CLAUDE.md, but loads **only** when Claude touches a matching file.
- `.claude/rules/r-code-conventions.md` with `paths: "*.R"` → loads only when an `.R` file is in play.
- Keeps CLAUDE.md focused on universal stuff. Specialty knowledge waits for its moment.
:::

## A rule example {.compact}

```yaml
# .claude/rules/replication-protocol.md
---
paths: ["scripts/**/*.R", "scripts/**/*.do", "scripts/**/*.py"]
---

# Replication-First R Code

When asked to extend an analysis:
1. FIRST: replicate the original numbers in the manuscript.
2. THEN: extend.
3. NEVER: ship a script that produces numbers that don't match the paper.

If the original numbers don't replicate, STOP and report what differs.
```

. . .

This rule auto-loads when Claude opens any `.R` / `.do` / `.py` file. Outside that scope, the rule isn't in context — staying lean.

## Hooks: deterministic enforcement {.compact}

CLAUDE.md and rules are **advisory** — Claude reads them and (usually) follows them.

. . .

**Hooks** are scripts that fire deterministically at lifecycle events. They don't ask Claude — they enforce.

::: {.incremental}
- `PreToolUse` — before any tool runs (block dangerous patterns)
- `PostToolUse` — after tool completes (sanity check, log, notify)
- `Stop` — when Claude is about to stop responding (verify, remind)
- `PreCompact` — before context auto-compaction (save plan to disk)
:::

## A hook example {.compact}

```python
# .claude/hooks/log-reminder.py — Stop hook
# Fires when Claude is about to stop responding.
# If session log hasn't been updated in N responses, remind to update it.

if responses_since_last_log_update > 50:
    print("Session log is stale. Update quality_reports/session_logs/", file=sys.stderr)
```

. . .

This isn't AI judgment — it's a Python script. Deterministic. Runs every Stop, no exceptions.

## What's coming next {.compact}

::: {.incremental}
- **Part 2** (next, 30 min): see all of the above run end-to-end on a real manuscript section.
- **Part 4** (later, 25 min): when multi-agent helps, when it hurts.
- **Part 5 + 6** (after lunch): the headline demos — replication audit + simulated peer review.
:::

. . .

Take a 30-second stretch. We're going to a live demo.

---

# Part 2 — End-to-End Demo {.emoryblue #part-2}

## What you're about to see {.compact}

::: {.incremental}
- A real working-paper introduction. ~3 paragraphs. Has known issues (one citation misattribution, one awkward sentence, one missing covariate mention).
- I ask Claude: *"Improve the introduction of this paper."*
- We watch the **full lifecycle** — plan → spec → approve → implement → multi-agent review → fix → verify → score.
:::

## What to watch for, not what to listen to {.compact}

The next 25 minutes is **infrastructure**, not prose.

::: {.incremental}
- I'll narrate at three checkpoints: **plan appears**, **review fans out**, **score reports**.
- The agent's edit suggestions matter less than **how the agent arrived at them**.
- If Claude gets one of the edits wrong, that's *good* — you'll see the review loop catch it.
:::

## The full lifecycle (preview) {.compact}

| Step | What happens | Time mark |
|------|-------------|-----------|
| 1 | I paste the prompt. Claude enters plan mode. | T+0 |
| 2 | Plan appears. I narrate. | T+3 |
| 3 | I approve. Implementation begins. | T+5 |
| 4 | Agent reads, edits, self-verifies. | T+6 |
| 5 | **Multi-agent review fans out** (proofreader + domain-reviewer + claim-verifier). | T+12 |
| 6 | Review reports surface. | T+18 |
| 7 | Fixes applied. | T+21 |
| 8 | Quality score, summary, done. | T+25 |

## What I want you to notice {.compact}

::: {.incremental}
1. **Plan mode is read-only.** Claude can't edit until I approve. This is the safety boundary.
2. **The review fanout is parallel.** Three agents in three contexts — no cross-contamination.
3. **The verifier never sees my prompt.** It checks the work in a fresh context (Chain-of-Verification, Dhuliawala et al. 2023).
4. **The score is from a deterministic Python script**, not the agent's self-evaluation. Numerical, reproducible.
:::

## To the editor {auto-animate=true}

::: {.callout-tip}
## Live demo

Switching to the IDE + terminal. Watch the screen, not the slides.

Demo script: `demos/01-end-to-end/README.md`. Pre-recorded fallback at `demos/01-end-to-end/pre-recorded.mp4` (5 min, edited).

Abort trigger: if **review fanout** hasn't fired by T+15, kill the live demo and play the recording.
:::

> [Pedro switches to the IDE. Live demo runs ~25 min.]

## Coming back from the demo {.compact}

. . .

What you just saw — in one sentence:

> **A 3-paragraph edit task became a 7-step structured workflow that produced an audit trail.**

## What got produced {.compact}

::: {.incremental}
- A **plan** (saved to `quality_reports/plans/`)
- A **spec** with MUST/SHOULD/MAY annotations (saved to `quality_reports/specs/`)
- The **edited manuscript section**
- **Three review reports** (one per agent), saved to `quality_reports/`
- A **quality score** (numerical, reproducible)
- A **session log** (what was done, by whom — useful for collaborators)
:::

. . .

Six artifacts from one prompt.

## Why this matters for B-school work {.compact}

::: {.incremental}
- **R&R round 2 = "what did I change since round 1?"** This audit trail answers it automatically.
- **Co-author collaboration**: the next person opens the spec + session log and resumes without a 30-min sync call.
- **Replication packages**: the same lifecycle generates them on the analysis side (Part 5).
:::

## What this didn't do {.compact}

Honesty matters here.

::: {.incremental}
- ❌ **It didn't write a better paper than I would have.** The agent's edits are *suggestions*, not authority.
- ❌ **It didn't catch every issue.** One subtle error is best caught by a reading mind, not a fast one.
- ✅ **It caught most of the surface stuff in 25 minutes**, freeing me to spend the next hour on *substance*.
:::

. . .

The point isn't replacement. It's **leverage on the boring 70%**.

## What we cover next {.compact}

We just saw the full lifecycle.

::: {.incremental}
- **Part 3 (next, 30 min):** what's a *skill*? How do you write your own? You'll sketch one.
- **Part 4 (after break, 25 min):** the orchestration story — when does multi-agent help, when does it hurt?
- **Part 5 + 6 (after lunch):** the two headline demos — replication audit + simulated peer review.
:::

---

# Part 3 — Building Reusable Skills {.claudeorange #part-3}

## What is a skill, really? {.compact}

::: {.incremental}
- A **skill** is a Markdown file at `.claude/skills/<name>/SKILL.md`.
- It has YAML frontmatter (when does Claude trigger it?) and a body (what to do).
- It auto-loads when a relevant prompt is detected — or you invoke it explicitly with `/<name>`.
- That's it. **No code, no installation, no plugin system.**
:::

. . .

A skill is just a written instruction — but a *triggerable, version-controlled, reusable* one.

## What a skill is NOT {.compact}

::: {.incremental}
- ❌ A plugin (no compilation, no extension API)
- ❌ A chat template (skills can read files, run code, call other skills)
- ❌ A prompt library entry (those are static; skills run *workflows*)
- ❌ Magic (you'll see exactly what's in one in 90 seconds)
:::

## Anatomy of a skill {.compact}

```yaml
---
name: my-skill-name
description: One-sentence description with trigger keywords. "Use when user says X, Y, Z."
argument-hint: "[optional flags / inputs]"
allowed-tools: ["Read", "Write", "Bash", "Grep", "Glob"]
disable-model-invocation: false   # set true if this skill must be user-explicit only
---

# My Skill Name

## Workflow

### Phase 1 — Inputs
Read the spec, validate inputs, refuse if results are present.

### Phase 2 — Generate
Produce the output, MUST/SHOULD/MAY annotated.

### Phase 3 — Verify
Cross-check against domain rules.

### Phase 4 — Output
Write to disk. Report to user.
```

. . .

That's a real skill template. Frontmatter ≤ 6 fields. Body = numbered phases.

## Why this beats writing the workflow into your prompts {.compact}

::: {.incremental}
- **Reusable** — write once, invoke anytime, share with co-authors
- **Versionable** — `git diff` your skill the way you `git diff` your code
- **Auditable** — anyone reading your repo sees the workflow as text
- **Composable** — a skill can invoke another skill (e.g., `/preregister` calls `/verify-claims`)
:::

# Walk-through: `/preregister` {.claudeorange}

## What it does (one slide) {.compact}

`/preregister` drafts a registry-ready preregistration document in your choice of style:

- **OSF** (default) — full template, social-science default
- **AsPredicted** — 9-question short form
- **AEA RCT Registry** — required for AEA-journal field experiments since 2018

. . .

It refuses to draft if your description contains *results* — preregistration is forward-looking by definition.

## Live walkthrough: the actual SKILL.md {auto-animate=true}

> [Pedro opens `~/Documents/GitHub/claude-code-my-workflow/.claude/skills/preregister/SKILL.md`.
> Walks the frontmatter (description, argument-hint, allowed-tools), then the 6 phases.
> Then INVOKES it on a fake study description.
> Audience sees: input → output, end to end, ~3 minutes.]

::: {.callout-tip}
## Demo prompt (verbatim)

> *I'm planning a 2x2 between-subjects survey experiment on N=800 US adults via Prolific. Manipulating message-source partisanship × policy framing on attitudes toward climate policy. Primary DV is a 4-item attitude index. Hypothesis: in-party + gain framing produces highest support. Pre-registered analysis: two-way ANOVA. Run /preregister --style aspredicted on this.*
:::

## Why this skill, for this audience {.compact}

::: {.incremental}
- **Pre-registration is the #1 referee ask** in finance experiments (JF, RFS) and accounting field/lab work (TAR, JAR, JAE).
- **AEA RCT Registry** is mandatory for AEA-journal field interventions; many B-school field studies don't realize they qualify.
- **The MUST/SHOULD/MAY annotation discipline** is exactly the framing reviewers expect — directional hypothesis, named estimator, ex-ante exclusions, sample-size stopping rule.
:::

## Three things to notice in the SKILL.md {.compact}

::: {.incremental}
1. **Trigger phrases in the description** — "preregister", "OSF", "AsPredicted", "AEA RCT", "PAP", "preanalysis plan". Why so many? See *Effective triggers* below.
2. **`disable-model-invocation: true`** — this skill writes a *commitment document*; it must be user-intent.
3. **Cross-check phase** — refuses to mark "ready" if hypothesis isn't directional, estimator isn't named, exclusions aren't ex-ante.
:::

## Effective trigger descriptions {.compact}

A skill triggers when its `description` matches the user's prompt. So the description must contain the words your future self / your co-author would actually use.

::: {.incremental}
- ❌ `description: A preregistration tool.` — too generic; never triggers
- ❌ `description: Pre-registers a study.` — singular, formal; misses real usage
- ✅ `description: Draft a preregistration document (OSF, AsPredicted, AEA RCT Registry style). Use when user says "preregister", "draft a preregistration", "OSF preregistration", "AsPredicted", "AEA RCT registry", "PAP", "preanalysis plan".`
:::

. . .

The description IS the trigger. Pack it with the actual phrases people use.

# Hands-on: sketch your own skill {.claudeorange}

## Your turn (10 minutes) {.compact}

::: {.incremental}
- Each handout has a 1-page **skill template** + 6 sample use cases (finance, accounting, econ, teaching, admin).
- Pick **one** use case (or your own).
- Fill in: name, description (with trigger phrases!), 3-step workflow.
- Don't try to make it run. Just draft.
- We'll workshop 1-2 volunteers' sketches at the end.
:::

. . .

**You have until [time].** Go.

## Sample use cases (pick one) {.compact}

::: {.incremental}
1. **Finance — JF submission formatter.** Standardize tables: clustered SE, sig stars per JF style, 3-decimal coefficients, observation count, R².
2. **Accounting — restatement matcher.** Given an Audit Analytics export, produce a sample-construction memo with exclusion-by-exclusion counts.
3. **Econ — referee-letter parser.** Map each numbered concern in a referee report to a specific revision file/line.
4. **Teaching — grading rubric applier.** Apply a fixed rubric to a student submission; produce a per-criterion score + comment.
5. **Admin — letter-of-support template.** Given a student name + program + my prior knowledge of them, draft a structured recommendation letter.
6. **Your own.** Anything you do at least 3 times a year.
:::

## What makes a good first skill {.compact}

::: {.incremental}
- **Repetitive task** — you do it more than 3 times a year
- **Has a structure** — there's a "right way" you can specify
- **Has a verifiable output** — you'll know if the agent got it wrong
- **Is small** — 1-3 phases. The first skill is the smallest skill that's useful.
:::

. . .

Most failures: trying to cram too much into skill #1. Aim small.

## Workshop (5 minutes) {.compact}

::: {.callout-tip}
## Volunteers

Two volunteers share their sketches. I read aloud + react in real time. We type one of them up live as a real `SKILL.md` to demonstrate the sketch → working-skill translation.

If no volunteers: I have two pre-drafted I can walk through (one finance, one accounting).
:::

# Design principles {.claudeorange}

## Reusability

::: {.incremental}
- **Specific enough to do something useful**, **general enough to apply more than once**.
- Trap: writing a skill that only solves *this paper's* problem. Re-frame to the *class of problems*.
- Test: would your co-author / next-year-you actually invoke this?
:::

## Specificity (in the description AND the body) {.compact}

::: {.incremental}
- **In the description**: trigger phrases people actually say, examples of when to use it, when *not* to use it.
- **In the body**: literal commands, exact filenames, named estimators. "Run an analysis" → no. "Run `Rscript scripts/02_main.R` and read the output of `Sys.time()`" → yes.
:::

## Built-in verification {.compact}

The best skills don't just *do* — they *check*.

::: {.incremental}
- `/preregister` refuses to mark MUST sections complete if hypothesis isn't directional.
- `/audit-reproducibility` (Part 5) compares numerical claims to script outputs with tolerance thresholds.
- `/checkpoint` reads `git status` to verify state before writing the snapshot.
:::

. . .

A skill without a verification step is a fancy macro. A skill *with* one is a workflow.

## Cost-of-creation, honestly {.compact}

::: {.incremental}
- A first skill: **30-60 minutes** to draft, test, iterate.
- A polished skill: **2-4 hours** including verification, examples, edge-case handling.
- A production skill (used by colleagues): **half a day** for full doc + tests.
:::

. . .

If you're spending less than 30 min, you're under-investing in the description. If more than 4 hours on draft #1, you're over-engineering.

## Cumulative leverage {.compact}

::: {.incremental}
- Year 1: 5 skills. Each saves 1 hour/month. Net: 60 hours/year freed.
- Year 2: 12 skills. Each saves 1-2 hours/month. Net: 200+ hours.
- Year 3: 20+. Skills start *invoking each other*. Net: hard to measure — your default workflow becomes "Claude + my repo of skills".
:::

. . .

The leverage compounds because **each skill is a written commitment** to *how you do this kind of work*. Future-you can read it.

## Take a 10-minute break {.emoryblue}

. . .

Back at [time]. Bathroom + coffee + check email.

. . .

After the break: **orchestration** — when does multi-agent help, when does it hurt?

---

# Part 4 — Orchestration + Multi-Agent {.claudeorange #part-4}

## Coming in Part 4

::: {.callout-note}
## Placeholder

Why orchestration → when agents help (parallel / critic-fixer / fresh-context) → when they don't → mini-demo of `/qa-quarto` + hooks.
:::

---

# Part 5 — Research + Replication {.emoryblue #part-5}

## Coming in Part 5

::: {.callout-note}
## Placeholder

**Finance/Accounting headline.** Replication-first framing → code inventory + comparison → AEA-style packaging → **live demo:** replication audit. Choreography in `demos/04-replication/README.md`.
:::

---

# Part 6 — Writing, Refereeing, Review Simulation {.emoryblue #part-6}

## Coming in Part 6

::: {.callout-note}
## Placeholder

**The headliner demo.** Referee perspectives → **live demo:** `/review-paper --peer JF` → drafting referee responses → **live demo:** `/respond-to-referees`. Choreography in `demos/05-peer-review/README.md`.
:::

---

# Part 7 — Closing {.claudeorange #part-7}

## Coming in Part 7

::: {.callout-note}
## Placeholder

The full stack visual recap → cross-tool strategies (Claude vs Codex audit) → ecosystem tour → 3 take-home actions.
:::

---

# Thank You {.emoryblue}

## Three things to do next week

1. **Clone `Claude-Mini`** ([github.com/pedrohcgs/Claude-Mini](https://github.com/pedrohcgs/Claude-Mini)) — these slides plus all demo materials.
2. **Fork `claude-code-my-workflow`** ([github.com/pedrohcgs/claude-code-my-workflow](https://github.com/pedrohcgs/claude-code-my-workflow)) — the full template I built this on.
3. **Write a CLAUDE.md** for your next paper. 30 minutes, biggest leverage.

## Stay in touch

- Email: pedro.santanna@emory.edu
- GitHub: [pedrohcgs](https://github.com/pedrohcgs)
- Web: [psantanna.com](https://psantanna.com)
